motivation: Understanding base constructs for setup & architecture foundations.
whyExists: Classical programming requires explicit rules for every scenario. ML handles problems where rules are: too complex to write (image recognition), change too fast to maintain (spam detection), unknown (drug discovery). ML powers: search engines, recommendation systems, fraud detection, medical diagnosis, autonomous vehicles, voice assistants. Understanding ML fundamentals is increasingly expected of all software engineers, not just data scientists.
realWorldAnalogy:
Imagine you're teaching a child to recognize cats. You don't give them a rulebook ("cats have whiskers, pointy ears, meow"). Instead, you show them thousands of pictures: "Cat. Cat. Dog. Cat. Dog." Over time, they learn to recognize cats on their own.

Machine Learning works the same way. You show the computer MANY examples with answers, and it figures out the rules itself. The more examples, the smarter it gets.
simpleExplanation: To learn Machine Learning Core Principles Part 1A from first principles, start by looking at how data resolves at runtime. Imagine your processor executing instructions sequentially. Machine Learning Core Principles Part 1A defines how values bind, operations evaluate, and stack structures are clean.
visualDiagram:
```text
[User Input] ──► [Process Scope: Machine Learning Core Principles Part 1A] ──► [Memory Address] ──► [Console Output]
```
